result:
The first light through the cracked bedroom window lands on the coffee mug I forgot to bring to the kitchen last night, turning the ring of dried grounds into a miniature mountain range, shadows deep enough to hide imaginary creatures. I lie still and watch it as if the sun had arranged this moment especially for me, a private topographical performance, and I think: this is how every day begins, with a quiet conspiracy between light and ordinary objects. We call it “morning,” but really it is a slow-motion magic trick that converts the familiar into the uncanny simply by tilting it eleven degrees to the left. My brain, still half soaked in dreams, does not yet know the names of the day’s obligations; it only recognizes shapes and textures, the way the blanket’s fuzz catches the light like frost on late-season peaches. In this liminal haze I am allowed to love the world without understanding it, the way a child loves the ocean before learning the word “drown.”

When I finally stand, the floorboards memorize the weight and cadence of my feet, adding another entry to a decades-long data set that will outlive me. Houses keep their own secret ledgers: who paced at 3 a.m., who ran to answer a ringing phone, who tiptoed in carrying shoes at dawn. One day someone else will walk here and never know they are stepping on the ghost arithmetic of my insomniac nights. I find comfort in that continuity, the same way I comfort myself on airplanes by imagining the invisible river of metal birds before and after mine, all of us drafting on the same jet-streamed breath. Continuity is a life jacket stitched from anonymity; we slip it on and pretend we are never truly alone.

Coffee, water, beans, heat: four words that contain centuries of empire, slavery, revolution, and comfort rituals. I grind the beans and think of the farmer in Huehuetenango who picked the cherry, the exporter who tasted parchment coffee and spoke the shorthand of altitude and process, the dockworker who tightened a container latch in the rain. All of those hands are riding shotgun in my kitchen at 6:42 a.m., though they will never know it. I wonder what they look forward to drinking at daybreak, whether they, too, stand barefoot on cool tile and listen for the first gurgle of water finding its way through ground mountains. The kettle hisses like it’s trying to pronounce my name but keeps stuttering on the consonants.

While the coffee steeps, I open the back door and taste the air the way an animal tastes wind: metallic with autumn, threaded with woodsmoke and tire rubber, a soup of leaf rot and possibility. The neighbor’s cat—named either Orion or Surly, depending on whom you ask—sits on the fence like a gargoyle assigned to guard against nostalgia. He blinks once, twice, a shutter capturing the last image of me before I change into whatever the day will require. I salute him with my mug; he yawns, unimpressed by ceremonies that do not involve tuna. Somewhere in the distance a circular saw screams through lumber, the sound of someone else’s future room being built. I imagine the sawdust drifting into a new arrangement of constellations on the plywood floor, a temporary galaxy that will be swept away once the workers break for lunch.

Work waits for me inside the rectangle of my laptop screen, a cinema of spreadsheets and politely urgent messages that flicker like subtitles for a film I never auditioned to appear in. Yet I play my role, typing responses that balance friendliness with efficiency, a tightrope walker carrying a backpack full of other people’s expectations. Between emails I open a browser tab and read a scientific article about the way trees communicate through fungal networks, how a dying stump can gift its carbon to a neighboring sapling for decades. The phrase “mother trees” lodges in my throat like a stone I’m afraid to swallow or spit out. I glance out the window at the lone maple in the yard; its leaves have not yet turned, but they hold that anticipation, the way a held breath waits for permission to exhale. I whisper thank you, though I’m not sure for what, or to whom the invoice should be sent.

By noon the sky has assembled a committee of clouds that can’t decide on minutes or motions. Sunlight alternates with shadow so quickly that the kitchen lights flicker as if the house itself had a mood disorder. I eat last night’s lentils while standing, flipping through a cookbook whose photographs portray bowls of stew as deep as meteor craters. I read the headnotes the way other people read horoscopes: “This soup is reminiscent of misty walks along the Connemara coast.” Someone was paid to write that sentence; someone else believed it enough to buy the book. I wonder if the recipe tester cried while seasoning broth, overwhelmed by the memory of fog on their shoulders, or if they just measured salt with scientific detachment and went home to watch reality television. Both versions feel true, which is probably the definition of commerce.

In the afternoon I walk to the river because moving my legs is the only reliable method I’ve found for translating restlessness into geometry. Along the path, black-eyed Susans have arranged themselves like enthusiastic spectators at a parade that never quite starts. A man jogs past pushing a stroller containing not a child but a shaggy dog who wears goggles and a bandana printed with tiny tacos. The man nods; the dog looks through me, focused on some canine horizon where every fire hydrant dispenses gravy. I laugh out loud, a sound so sudden it startles a sparrow into a trajectory that reminds me of childhood handwriting, all loops and urgency. The river itself is low, exposing ribs of sediment that make the water appear guilty of something. I sit on a bench donated by the family of “Martha L., who loved this view.” I say her name out loud, a small resurrection that costs me nothing.

An email pings: a reminder that my data-storage subscription will renew tomorrow unless I cancel. I imagine all my uploaded photographs drifting like rubber ducks in a server farm cooled by giant fans. One picture among them shows a younger me on a different riverbank, cheeks sunburned, holding a fish that was released seconds later. That fish is perhaps still out there, or more likely it has become another fish, then a bird, then soil, then grape, then wine, then human song. I picture the data version of that moment being dusted by magnetic storms in a warehouse outside Des Moines, a second death no elegy will ever reach. I leave the subscription active; $2.99 a month seems a bargain for postponing oblivion.

Evening arrives disguised as the smell of grilled onions and the sound of a basketball being dribbled by someone who believes practice is the same as prayer. I cook without a recipe, chopping onions until my eyes write their own sad manifestos. The sizzle is a standing ovation for the fact that I have survived another day without inventing a new regret. While the vegetables soften, I play music chosen by an algorithm that claims to know me. The first song is one I loved in college; the second is by an artist who overdosed last winter. I think of code sorting grief by tempo, major keys filed under “resilient,” minor keys under “introspective, may pair well with rain.” The playlist is a séance conducted by engineers, and it works: I am visited by who I was at nineteen, barefoot in a dorm hallway, certain that longing was a credential.

After dinner I read a novel in which nothing happens except that two people almost fall in love for two hundred pages then decide against it in the final chapter. The sentences are so beautiful I have to close the book every few minutes to stare at the wall, as if the plaster could teach me how to bear beauty that refuses to resolve. Somewhere in those paragraphs is the scent of citrus groves at dusk, but the characters never taste the fruit; they only talk about the possibility of tasting it, which is apparently more exquisite than sugar. I wonder if we are all like that, forever orbiting the thing itself, convinced that description is richer than experience. My phone buzzes with a headline about distant war, and the question feels obscene in its immediacy. I turn the device face-down, a small rebellion against the gravity of elsewhere.

At bedtime I open the window so the night can enter with its pockets full of rustling. Crickets trade gossip in Morse code; a car door slams like a brief argument that thought better of itself. I try to catalog the day—I made coffee, answered emails, walked to the river, cooked, read—but the list feels like counting ripples instead of water. What returns instead is the image of the sunlit grounds in the forgotten mug, miniature mountains that have by now been washed away. I realize that I am, at every moment, both the ridge and the erosion, the witness and the weather that erases. That seems as close to a creed as I’m capable of holding: Be the topography and the finger tracing it; be the record and the rinsing sponge. Outside, Orion/Surly yowls once, a sound that could mean territory or loneliness or simply that the moon is full enough to deserve commentary. I answer with a whistle, off-key but sincere, and the dark absorbs both of our sentences into its long, unedited manuscript.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
